annotations.label: Numerical digit

anchor.snippet: is a single symbol used to represent numbers in positional notation

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes digit sequences via Nat.digits (little-endian list of base-b digits) but has no standalone notion of a digit as a symbol.

annotations.label: Number of digits equals base

anchor.snippet: the number of different digits required is the absolute value of the base

annotations.note: Nat.digits_lt_base proves every base-b digit is < b (so values range 0..b-1), but the cardinality claim 'number of distinct digits = base' is not stated.

annotations.label: Digits in decimal, binary, hexadecimal
annotations.note: These concrete base examples are not stated as lemmas in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Value of a numeral

anchor.snippet: The value of the numeral is computed by multiplying each digit in the sequence by its place value

annotations.note: Nat.ofDigits interprets a digit list as a number, with ofDigits_eq_sum_mapIdx giving the explicit sum of digit times place value.

annotations.label: Digits and the radix

anchor.snippet: has one unique digit for each integer from zero up to, but not including, the radix of the number system

annotations.note: Nat.digits_lt_base establishes that digits lie in 0..radix-1, but the radix-to-digit-set correspondence is not a separate definition.

annotations.label: Place values in 12 and 312

anchor.snippet: The number 12 is expressed with the numeral

annotations.note: This is a specific worked numeric example, not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Successive place values

anchor.snippet: Each successive place to the left of this has a place value equal to the place value of the previous digit times the base

annotations.note: The place value b^i (each position scaled by an extra factor of b) is implicit in ofDigits_eq_sum_mapIdx, but 'place value' is not a named defined concept.

annotations.label: Place value formula

anchor.snippet: The calculation involves the multiplication of the given digit by the base raised by the exponent

annotations.note: Nat.ofDigits_eq_sum_mapIdx states ofDigits b L = sum of a * b^i, exactly the digit-times-base-power place value formula.

annotations.label: Place values in 10.34
annotations.note: Fractional/decimal place values are not handled by Nat.digits, which only treats natural numbers.

annotations.label: Balanced ternary

anchor.snippet: Balanced ternary is unusual in having the digit values

annotations.note: No balanced ternary representation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Digital root

anchor.snippet: The digital root is the single-digit number obtained by summing the digits of a given number

annotations.note: Digit-sum infrastructure and n ≡ (digits 10 n).sum [MOD 9] exist, but the iterated single-digit digital root function itself is not defined.

annotations.label: Casting out nines

anchor.snippet: Casting out nines is a procedure for checking arithmetic done by hand

annotations.note: Nat.modEq_nine_digits_sum formalizes the underlying congruence n ≡ digit-sum mod 9, but the arithmetic-checking procedure is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Digital root check for addition

anchor.snippet: Casting out nines makes use of the fact that if

annotations.note: The fact n ≡ digit-sum mod 9 (modEq_nine_digits_sum) plus Nat.ModEq's compatibility with addition derives the check, but it is not stated as a single lemma.

annotations.label: Repunit

anchor.snippet: Repunits are integers that are represented with only the digit 1

annotations.note: No repunit definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 1111 is a repunit

anchor.snippet: 1111 (one thousand, one hundred and eleven) is a repunit

annotations.note: No repunit concept or this example exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Repdigit

anchor.snippet: Repdigits are a generalization of repunits

annotations.note: No repdigit definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 333 is a repdigit
annotations.note: No repdigit concept or this example exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Palindromic number

anchor.snippet: Palindromic numbers are numbers that read the same when their digits are reversed

annotations.note: List.Palindrome defines lists equal to their reverse and is applied to (digits 10 n) in eleven_dvd_of_palindrome, but there is no dedicated 'palindromic number' definition.

annotations.label: Lychrel number

anchor.snippet: A Lychrel number is a positive integer that never yields a palindromic number

annotations.note: No Lychrel number or reverse-and-add iteration is formalized in Mathlib.
